description: Datacenter opposition news research for March 26-28, 2026. Updates to existing entries and new fights discovered via web search.

status_updates.update_note: ZBA advanced the proposed 12-month moratorium on data centers with 10,000+ sq ft processing area. A public hearing was held March 12 at the Shields-Carter Meeting Room. The measure next goes to the Environmental Land Use Committee (potentially April 9) before a full County Board vote later in April. A Data Center Activities Task Force has been created to develop zoning/permitting standards. The full county board has NOT yet voted on the moratorium.

HB 4084 PASSED the Oregon Legislature before sine die on March 6, 2026. Governor Kotek succeeded in passing the bill, which expands Enterprise Zone property tax breaks but includes an amendment blocking new data center projects from eligibility until summer 2027. Kotek's Data Center Advisory Committee (formed January 2026, met once) will issue recommendations before the long 2027 session. Kotek's office said it will 'address immediate concerns.' The bill has been signed by the governor. Status should change to enacted.

ESSB 6231 enrolled March 13 and delivered to Governor Ferguson for signature. The bill repeals the sales tax exemption for data center equipment refurbishment/replacement at existing facilities effective July 1, 2026, while preserving exemptions for new facilities. Revenue: $63M this biennium, $140.5M next biennium. Ferguson requested the bill via OFM. No formal signing confirmation found as of March 28, but Ferguson is expected to sign since it was his proposal and is part of the supplemental budget. Status remains enacted (already enrolled).

Sanders-AOC AI Data Center Moratorium Act officially INTRODUCED on March 25, 2026. Massive media coverage: PBS, WaPo, Axios, Common Dreams, Democracy Now, Roll Call, The Hill. The bill conditions new data center construction on passage of federal laws regulating AI, energy costs, and environmental effects. Food & Water Watch endorsed, citing over 230 orgs in support letter from December. Opposition swift: ITIF/Center for Data Innovation called it unjustified; Sen. Fetterman (D-PA) rejected it, agreeing with Interior Sec. Burgum that a moratorium is a 'surrender flag' to China. No named co-sponsors beyond Sanders and AOC. Extremely unlikely to advance. Context: at least 54 local governments have passed freezes; 12+ state moratorium bills filed.

Tulsa City Council voted UNANIMOUSLY on March 25 to approve the 9-month moratorium on new data center construction (amended from original 365-day proposal by Councilor Laura Bellis). Moratorium runs through Dec 31, 2026 or until zoning rules updated. Exempts Project Anthem phases 1-2 and Project Clydesdale. Planning office will provide 60-day updates. 19 speakers supported during public comment. Already recorded in fights.json with correct status.

Hanover County Planning Commission DEFERRED the Mountain Road Tech Park proposal AGAIN on March 19, 2026 -- the second consecutive deferral. This time it was the applicants (Tract) who requested another month to address questions about traffic impacts and buffer sizes. Large community turnout continued despite no vote. Next hearing scheduled for April 16, 2026.

MAJOR DEVELOPMENT: Archbald Borough Council DENIED Archbald 1 LLC's conditional use application for an 18-building data center campus during a special Friday afternoon meeting on March 27, 2026. All five council members voted unanimously to deny the permit. The proposed campus was on 400 acres near Staback Park. Over 200 residents packed the meeting and cheered the denial. This follows a March 19 regime change on council, where the president, vice president, and president pro tem were ousted by four other members who said the former leaders were not acting in the borough's best interest regarding data centers. A legal notice error (Times-Tribune) forced the decision meeting to be rescheduled. This is the FIRST of six proposed data center campuses in Archbald to receive a formal denial.

Related to Wildcat Ridge entry above. The broader Archbald fight saw a council regime change on March 19 (president, VP, president pro tem ousted). PPL Electric Utilities announced open house on planned transmission upgrades near proposed Lackawanna County data center campuses (March 25). Spotlight PA published a major investigative piece on Archbald's data center rush. The legal challenge to the original zoning amendment continues separately.

A final hearing in the rezoning lawsuit was scheduled for March 19, 2026 at 10 AM before Judge David J. Hobdy in Courtroom 340 at the Bessemer Justice Center. The plaintiffs (Havron, Buchanan, Morgan) argue the 700-acre agricultural-to-industrial rezoning is arbitrary spot zoning. The City filed a Renewed Motion to Dismiss. NO ruling found in search results as of March 28 -- the outcome of the March 19 hearing is unknown. The Planning & Zoning Commission previously recommended an ADDITIONAL 914 acres for the project, bringing the total site to 1,600 acres.

The East Vincent Planning Commission voted to RECOMMEND DENIAL of the Pennhurst data center proposal in mid-February 2026, calling it 'technically deficient' and not in compliance with the zoning ordinance. The project now enters the conditional use hearing phase before the Board of Supervisors, scheduled to begin on March 16 at 6:30 PM at East Vincent Elementary School and potentially extending into April. The Philadelphia Inquirer published a major piece on March 24 about data centers eyeing Chester County collar counties. Spring City Borough Council took a stance against the project. The developer's attorney has stated they will not follow the township's draft ordinance (which was shelved) because it was too restrictive.

Sangamon County Board voted 15-13 on March 23 to TABLE the CyrusOne conditional use zoning permit. 700+ residents attended; speakers opposed the project over nearly 4 hours. The $500M project would be on 280 acres of farmland in Talkington Township. The Zoning Board of Appeals had recommended approval 5-0. NO new vote date has been scheduled as of March 28. Board meets again in April. Opposition focused on pollution, loss of farmland, electricity drain/rate increases, and wanting to wait for potential state-level regulations.

The Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee submitted 22,169 signatures (~10% of registered voters; only ~15,000 needed). The Board of Elections voted unanimously to direct the election director to count and validate signatures, with 20 days to complete the process. Validation is UNDERWAY but NOT YET COMPLETE as of March 28. Even if signatures are validated, the county attorney has argued the zoning decision may not qualify for a referendum under the county charter, meaning a legal challenge is likely. The referendum targets the County Council's creation of a 2,600-acre data center development zone.

Denver's proposed one-year data center moratorium could be formally introduced at a City Council meeting as early as March 31, 2026. Mayor Mike Johnston and council members intend to file the moratorium, which would pause building permits while the city reviews zoning, energy/water usage rules, and utility bill impacts. The moratorium will NOT stop future phases of CoreSite's Elyria-Swansea facility but will require them to follow any new regulations. Meanwhile, Colorado data center bills split environmental and labor groups: SB26-102 (Sen. Kipp, environmental regulations + BYONCE requirements) vs. HB26-1030 (industry-backed, 20-year tax exemptions in exchange for standards). Neither bill likely to pass in current form; negotiations ongoing.

REFERENDUM EFFORT KILLED BY COURT: On March 25, Judge Jennifer M. Phillips DENIED the declaratory judgment and injunctive relief petition filed by three Independence residents seeking to force a vote on the Nebius tax incentive package. The court ruled the ordinance authorizing up to $150.6 billion in revenue bonds took effect immediately under the city charter (Section 7.2 covering contracts and bond issuances), placing it outside the reach of a public referendum. Phillips also reversed a previous decision that had extended the signature collection deadline. Despite residents collecting thousands of signatures, the ruling effectively ended the referendum effort. The Nebius data center project moves forward without a public vote. Status for the community challenge should change to defeated (the underlying project is active).

Opposition continues to build. Online petition surpassed 1,200 signatures. A newly formed Facebook opposition group is gaining traction. Residents gathered at a community meeting and at local parks to distribute yard signs. Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy filed a lawsuit challenging the city's environmental review process. Meanwhile, Minnesota Legislature advancing data center transparency bills: one prohibiting local governments from entering NDAs, another requiring at least two public hearings and disclosure of key details. Lawmakers have bipartisan support.

data_center_watch_report.headline: $64 billion in U.S. data center projects blocked ($18B) or delayed ($46B) by community opposition over last two years
data_center_watch_report.details: At least 142 activist groups across 24 states organizing against data centers. 54 local governments have passed short-term freezes on data center construction. 12+ state moratorium bills filed in 2026 legislative sessions. Report published by Data Center Watch, backed by 10a Labs.

mountain_west_moratoriums.headline: Mountain West counties increasingly adopting data center moratoriums with bipartisan support
mountain_west_moratoriums.details: KVNF article (March 27): Kootenai County, Idaho lifted moratorium after banning data centers over the aquifer and requiring conditional use permits. Logan County, Colorado lifted moratorium after passing regulations. Data center regulation transcending party lines in traditionally conservative Mountain West counties.

colorado_legislative_split.headline: Colorado data center bills split environmental and labor groups
colorado_legislative_split.details: SB26-102 (Sen. Kipp, D-Fort Collins): BYONCE mandate requiring large data centers to use 100% renewable energy by 2031, plus prevailing wages. HB26-1030 (industry-backed): Creates Data Center Development Authority, 20-year sales tax exemptions in exchange for labor/efficiency standards. Environmental groups back SB26-102; labor unions lean toward HB26-1030 for construction job guarantees. Neither bill likely to pass in current form. Negotiations ongoing.

archbald_council_regime_change.headline: Archbald Borough Council underwent regime change over data center fight
archbald_council_regime_change.details: On March 19, four council members ousted the sitting president, vice president, and president pro tem for not acting in the borough's best interest regarding the six proposed data center campuses. The new council then denied the first data center application on March 27.

searches_with_no_new_results.bessemer_march_19_ruling: The March 19 hearing was scheduled but no ruling or outcome found in any search results as of March 28. May need to check Jefferson County Circuit Court records directly or wait for local media coverage.
searches_with_no_new_results.frederick_county_signature_validation: Signature validation process is underway (22,169 submitted, ~15,000 needed) but NO final result announced yet. 20-day count period ongoing.
searches_with_no_new_results.datacenterwatch_march_2026_report: No new March 2026 report from datacenterwatch.org. The $64B figure comes from their running report (updated periodically, not a dated March 2026 release). Most recent formal report was Q2 2025.
